claim: The high hapax ratio (70.1%) is largely an artifact of a systematic abbreviation or truncation cipher, where scribes consistently dropped word-final syllables or morphemes, causing each abbreviated form to appear unique even though the underlying vocabulary is much smaller.
evidence_for: Hapax ratio of 70.1% far exceeds natural language norms (typically 40-55% for medieval Latin manuscripts of comparable size)
Average word length of 5.03 glyphs is shorter than expected for unabbreviated Latin or Italian
Word-final glyph distribution is highly constrained (y, n, l, r, o dominate), consistent with a small set of suffix substitutes
evidence_against: Zipf exponent 0.8946 with R-squared 0.9084 indicates moderate but not strong natural-language Zipf fit, which genuine abbreviation should push closer to 1.0
If abbreviation were systematic, hapax forms should cluster near specific word lengths, but avg length 5.03 suggests moderate spread
Text-only section entropy 3.9016 bits is highest, inconsistent with heavy abbreviation flattening variance
test: Compute the hapax word-length distribution and compare to non-hapax word-length distribution. If abbreviation drives hapax inflation, hapax words should be systematically shorter than recurring words.
test_metric: Mean word length of hapax legomena vs. mean word length of words appearing 2+ times; also compute KL divergence between the two length distributions.
test_threshold: Confirmation: hapax mean length < recurring mean length by >0.5 glyphs and KL divergence > 0.15 nats. Denial: lengths are statistically indistinguishable (p > 0.05 by Mann-Whitney U).

tests_run.test: compute the hapax word-length distribution and compare to non-hapax word-length distribution. if abb
tests_run.details: Observed hapax ratio: 0.701. Expected: ~0.50. Voynich has unusually high hapax ratio (70.1%), suggesting either large vocabulary, verbose encoding, or noise.

reasoning: If abbreviation drives hapax inflation, decipherment can focus on reconstructing root forms rather than treating each hapax as a distinct lexical item, dramatically narrowing the solution space.
